summary: In the summer of 1964, I volunteered for Freedom Summer and spent most of my time in Greenwood, Mississippi. It was a dangerous place, with several civil-rights workers killed that summer, but it was also where I got arrested three times and experienced firsthand the hostility from white folks. Mississippi was the battleground for voter registration drives and civil rights activism, and I witnessed the courage of local people like Fannie Lou Hamer who faced violent retaliation for their efforts.

Freedom Summer in 1964 was the pivotal event that brought me to Mississippi as a volunteer. It was a time of great risk, with three civil-rights workers murdered and many others arrested, including myself. The campaign focused on registering black voters and challenging segregation, and it deeply shaped my involvement in the civil-rights movement. Sally Belfrage documented our experiences during Freedom Summer, which helped bring national attention to our struggle.

Sally Belfrage was in Greenwood, Mississippi, during Freedom Summer, writing about our experiences on the ground. She later published the book Freedom Summer in 1965, which included accounts of my arrests and the dangers we faced. Sally's work helped document the movement and brought wider awareness to the challenges and bravery of volunteers like me and others in Mississippi.

The book Freedom Summer, published in 1965 by Sally Belfrage, captured the realities of our work in Mississippi during that summer. It included stories about the arrests, threats, and violence we endured, including my own experiences with police harassment and jail. This book was an important record of the civil-rights struggle and helped spread the message of the movement beyond Mississippi.

During Freedom Summer, I worked closely with the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee, or SNCC, especially in Greenwood, Mississippi. The SNCC office was a hub for organizing voter registration drives and demonstrations, and it was where I was heading when I got arrested during a teenage march. SNCC was central to the movement's grassroots activism and played a key role in challenging segregation and disenfranchisement.

After many civil-rights activists, including myself, were arrested in Mississippi, our cases were moved to federal court to ensure fairness. However, the U.S. Supreme Court ultimately ruled against the civil-rights movement and sent the cases back to Mississippi courts, where they quietly disappeared. This was a disappointing setback for the movement, showing the challenges we faced even at the highest judicial levels.

After returning from Mississippi in August 1964, I continued my activism in New York City by supporting the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party challenge. I attended meetings and reached out to churches in Queens County to build support for the civil-rights struggle. New York City was a base for organizing and advocacy that connected local supporters to the movement in the South.

The Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party was a key focus of my activism after Freedom Summer. I worked to support their challenge to the all-white Mississippi delegation at the 1964 Democratic National Convention. The MFDP, led by figures like Fannie Lou Hamer, sought to expose the disenfranchisement of black voters and demand representation, and I helped rally support for their cause in New York City.

Mickey Schwerner was one of the civil-rights workers whose death marked the dangers of Freedom Summer in Mississippi. After returning to New York, I met his father, Nat Schwerner, who was also advocating for support of the MFDP challenge. Mickey's sacrifice was a powerful reminder of the risks we all faced in fighting for civil rights.

Nat Schwerner, Mickey Schwerner's father, was someone I met in New York City while working to support the MFDP challenge. He was deeply involved in seeking justice and support for the civil-rights movement after his son's tragic death in Mississippi. Our shared commitment to the cause connected us during that difficult time.

Fannie Lou Hamer was a remarkable and brave woman from northwest Mississippi whom I got to know during Freedom Summer. Despite violent attacks by the Ku Klux Klan and losing her job and home, she remained a fierce fighter for voting rights and civil rights. I heard her speak at Williams CME Church in Harlem in December 1964, where she inspired many with her spirit and powerful voice.

Williams CME Church in Harlem was the venue where I heard Fannie Lou Hamer speak in December 1964. It was a gathering place for civil-rights activists and supporters, including Malcolm X, who also spoke there that day. The church played an important role in rallying support for the MFDP challenge and raising awareness about the struggle in Mississippi.

Malcolm X spoke at Williams CME Church in Harlem in December 1964 alongside Fannie Lou Hamer. He called for a 'Mau-Mau in Mississippi,' which was seen as a violent statement by the press, but I understood it as his way of finding his role in the civil-rights movement. His fiery rhetoric aimed to push white Mississippians into supporting groups like SNCC and Martin Luther King Jr.

I went to Washington, D.C., on September 17, 1965, to witness the House of Representatives' roll-call vote on whether to unseat the all-white Mississippi delegation. The vote was a travesty, with many so-called liberals from New York City initially abstaining and only voting in favor when defeat seemed certain. It was a moment that revealed the hypocrisy and political compromises surrounding civil-rights issues in Congress.

I traveled to Washington, D.C., in September 1965 to attend the House of Representatives' vote on the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party challenge. It was a critical moment in the civil-rights movement, though the outcome was disappointing due to political maneuvering. Being there in the nation's capital underscored the national significance of the struggle for voting rights in Mississippi.

The Ku Klux Klan was a violent force opposing civil-rights activists in Mississippi, and I saw its impact firsthand. For example, after Fannie Lou Hamer tried to register to vote, the Klan shot through her home, and she and her husband were fired and evicted. Their terror tactics were meant to intimidate black citizens and activists, but they only strengthened the resolve of people like Fannie Lou and the movement as a whole.

In August 1964, I was aware of the Democratic National Convention in Atlantic City, New Jersey, where Lyndon B. Johnson expected an easy nomination. The Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party was there challenging the all-white delegation, seeking recognition and democracy. This event was a key moment in the civil-rights movement's political struggle.

Lyndon B. Johnson was the sitting president during the 1964 Democratic National Convention in Atlantic City, where he expected a smooth nomination. However, the MFDP's challenge to the Mississippi delegation disrupted his plans and exposed the ongoing fight for civil rights. Johnson's administration was a backdrop to many of the political battles we faced in the movement.

John Dittmer is the author of Local People, a book published in 1994 that tells the story of the Mississippi civil-rights struggle. I recommend his work because it provides a detailed and insightful account of the movement and the people involved, including the MFDP challenge and figures like Fannie Lou Hamer. His book helped me understand and share the history of those critical years.

Local People is a book by John Dittmer that I found very informative about the civil-rights movement in Mississippi. It covers the struggles of the MFDP and activists like Fannie Lou Hamer in great detail. This work helped me contextualize the events I witnessed and participated in during Freedom Summer and beyond.

Sojourners magazine published a piece in December 1982 that highlighted Fannie Lou Hamer's spiritual strength and leadership in the civil-rights movement. I recall reading about how she embraced the power of the Gospel and resisted hatred despite the oppression she faced. Sojourners helped preserve the legacy of activists like her and inspired continued commitment to justice.
